key_takeaways: Bharti Airtel is prioritizing deleveraging and financial prudence, demonstrated by the prepayment of high-cost spectrum dues.
Capex is expected to moderate in FY2026 as a percentage of revenues, driven by the completion of major 5G rollouts.
Free cash flow will be allocated towards deleveraging, increased dividends, and selective B2B investments.
Bharti Hexacom's net debt to EBITDAaL is at 1.03, with potential dividend payouts being considered alongside FWA rollout capex.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: The operating free cash flow, which is the EBITDAaL less capex, was strong, at about Rs.10,750 Crores. Capex for the quarter was about Rs.7200 Crores.
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: We delivered strong operating free cash flow; this is really EBITDAaL minus capex of 9440 Crores. Capex for the quarter was 6860 Crores.
metrics.language_shift: Operating free cash flow decreased slightly from Rs. 10,750 Crores to Rs. 9,440 Crores, and capex decreased from Rs. 7200 Crores to Rs. 6860 Crores.
metrics.validation_note: The new quote's numbers do not match the transcript. The correct quote is: "We delivered strong operating free cash flow; this is really EBITDAaL minus capex of 9440 Crores. Capex for the quarter was 6860 Crores."
metrics.market_note: The signal suggests a slight decrease in operating free cash flow. Without specific analyst commentary on the magnitude of this decrease and its impact, it's difficult to assess market alignment.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: Our balance sheet strength is predicated on disciplined capex and continued operational excellence. India net debt to EBITDAaL now stands at 1.32.
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: India net debt in relation to EBITDAaL now stands at 1.8 compared to 2.5 a year ago.
metrics.language_shift: India net debt to EBITDAaL increased to 1.8, but is compared to 2.5 a year ago, highlighting deleveraging efforts.
metrics.market_note: The signal indicates deleveraging efforts with India net debt to EBITDAaL improving to 1.8. This aligns with a general positive market view on debt reduction and improved financial health.

metrics.quote_old: Soumen Ray – Chief Financial Officer - India & South Asia – Bharti Airtel Limited & Director, Bharti Hexacom Limited: Yeah, Gopal, what you said is right. Basically, we have accelerated our home pass rollout as well as, we are deploying on 5G. Both the two avenues of growth are being triggered at the same time. You will see some amount of elevated capex in the near future because of both going parallelly. Thank you.
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: Our discipline capex spending, financial prudence, and sustained deleveraging reflects in our balance sheet. We believe that our capex for the current year will be lower than FY2024, and this will continue to unwind in FY2026.
metrics.language_shift: The narrative shifts from expecting elevated capex due to 5G and home pass rollout to anticipating moderating capex in FY2026.
metrics.market_note: The signal anticipates moderating capex in FY2026. Lower capex is generally viewed positively by the market as it frees up cash flow and improves profitability.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: During the quarter, we prepaid DoT spectrum dues pertaining to 2016. With this prepayment, we have cleared all the dues prior to 2021 auctions. In fact, in the last six quarters, we prepaid over 35,500 Crores of high cost spectrum dues. India net debt in relation to EBITDAaL now stands at 1.8 compared to 2.5 a year ago.
metrics.language_shift: Bharti Airtel highlights the prepayment of significant high-cost spectrum dues, showcasing a focus on deleveraging and financial prudence.
metrics.market_note: Prepayment of high-cost spectrum dues is a clear positive signal, indicating financial prudence and reduced interest burden. This aligns with a positive market reaction to deleveraging.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: Opportunities for free cash flow, I think, will be some amount of deleveraging, which will continue as you also pointed out. There will be a step-up of dividend for sure and that is a second area of free cash flow and the third, we will be selective and very prudent in any investments we make.
metrics.language_shift: Bharti Airtel outlines its strategy for free cash flow allocation, focusing on deleveraging, increased dividends, and selective investments.
metrics.market_note: The company explicitly outlines its priorities for free cash flow: deleveraging, increased dividends, and prudent investments. This provides more clarity on capital allocation strategy, which aligns with the market's preference for balanced capital allocation.

metrics.subtopic: Bharti Hexacom Net Debt to EBITDAaL and Dividend
metrics.quote_old: Soumen Ray – Chief Financial Officer - India & South Asia – Bharti Airtel Limited & Director, Bharti Hexacom Limited: Operating free cash generation, which is EBITDAaL minus capex, was a strong Rs.730 Crores. Our balance sheet is robust with a net debt excluding leases, of about Rs.2,814 Crores and a net debt excluding FLO versus EBITDAaL of about 0.6%.
metrics.quote_new: Soumen Ray – Chief Financial Officer - India & South Asia – Bharti Airtel Limited & Director, Bharti Hexacom Limited: Net debt to EBITDAaL improved to 1.03.
metrics.language_shift: Bharti Hexacom's net debt to EBITDAaL is explicitly stated as 1.03, and dividend payouts are being considered, alongside capex for FWA rollout.
metrics.validation_note: The old quote's net debt to EBITDAaL is incorrect. The correct quote is: "Operating free cash generation, which is EBITDAaL minus capex, was a strong Rs.730 Crores. Our balance sheet is robust with a net debt excluding leases, of about Rs.2,814 Crores and a net debt excluding FLO versus EBITDAaL of about 0.6."
metrics.market_note: A low net debt to EBITDAaL (1.03) and consideration of dividend payouts are positive signals for Bharti Hexacom, suggesting financial health and potential shareholder returns.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: For us, we see Indus as a very clearly undervalued asset, it is a strong dividend-paying Company, it is a vital infrastructure for us and therefore, given all of these reasons, we have taken up the stake there, or we have proposed, we have taken an enabling provision to take up the stake there and at some point based on how things play out, we will look to activate that provision.
metrics.quote_new: No longer discussed
metrics.language_shift: The discussion about increasing stake in Indus Towers is no longer present in the current quarter's transcript.
metrics.market_note: The absence of discussion about increasing stake in Indus Towers is classified as noise. Without knowing the market's prior expectations or views on this stake, it's difficult to validate.

key_takeaways: Bharti Airtel is focusing on acquiring quality customers in various segments and retooling its B2B segment by exiting low-margin businesses.
The company is expanding its FWA coverage and fiber home passes to increase its addressable market for Wi-Fi services.
While ARPU growth continues, driven by similar factors as the previous quarter, there's a stronger emphasis on the need for further tariff repair for financial stability.
Bharti Hexacom reported industry-leading ARPU growth and a rebound in net customer additions, contributing positively to the overall revenue growth.

metrics.quote_old: I do want to underscore that featurephone to Smartphone upgrades, prepaid to postpaid upgrades, data monetization, and international roaming remain key drivers of our ARPU growth in the absence of any tariff repair.
metrics.quote_new: ARPU drivers on an underlying basis remain intact; these are basically feature phone to smartphone upgrades, prepaid to postpaid upgrades, data monetization, and growth of international roaming.
metrics.language_shift: The drivers of ARPU growth remain consistent between the two quarters, with a slight rephrasing. The emphasis on these drivers continues in the absence of tariff repair.
metrics.market_note: Consistent ARPU growth drivers without tariff repair is classified as noise. It's difficult to assess market alignment without knowing the specific ARPU figures and analyst expectations.

metrics.quote_old: Whether you look at average revenue per user or rate per gigabyte, India is the lowest on both those axes across the world, so I think there is certainly room for more tariff repair, but I also think that the price architecture, if it changes, which is again a point I have made in the past, to a more sensible ladder of pricing from small, medium, large to extra large. You will see natural tailwind for ARPU because the people who can afford to pay will then pay a lot more and people who cannot afford to pay will not need to pay enough.
metrics.quote_new: Tariff repair is needed some more for the industry to be financially stable and deliver reasonable returns on a sustained basis.
metrics.language_shift: The narrative shifts from discussing the potential for tariff repair and a more sensible pricing architecture to a direct statement emphasizing the necessity of tariff repair for financial stability and reasonable returns. The tone is more urgent and direct in the current quarter.
metrics.market_note: The narrative shifts from a potential future tariff repair to a current need for tariff repair to ensure financial stability. The tone is more assertive about the necessity of tariff adjustments, which aligns with the market's understanding that tariff increases are crucial for improving profitability in the telecom sector.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: You will recall that a few years back, we did the same in our mobile business by shedding 49 million customers, who were using our network only for incoming calls. This changed the experience for our existing customers, it de-clogged our networks, and more importantly, it galvanized the organization internally on how we chose to win with quality customers. We believe this will do the same in our B2B portfolio.
metrics.language_shift: The company is now explicitly focusing on 'quality customers' and is willing to shed low-value customers to improve the overall customer experience and network efficiency. This is a new strategic emphasis.
metrics.market_note: A strategic emphasis on 'quality customers' and shedding low-value customers is generally viewed positively, as it improves overall customer experience and network efficiency, leading to higher ARPU.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: Airtel Business we delivered revenue of just under 5650 Crores. This is a growth of 8.7% over the same period last year. ... We believe this will do the same in our B2B portfolio.
metrics.language_shift: The company is actively 'retooling' its B2B segment, including exiting low-margin commodity voice and messaging businesses. This suggests a strategic shift towards higher-value B2B services.
metrics.market_note: Actively 'retooling' the B2B segment and exiting low-margin businesses is a positive strategic shift towards higher-value services, which the market is likely to favor.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: In the broadband segment, we added nearly 674,000 customers and stepped up our momentum in this business. We have expanded our FWA coverage and are now live in over 2000 cities. It is clear that FWA is increasing our addressable market for Wi-Fi services.
metrics.language_shift: The company is actively expanding its FWA (Fixed Wireless Access) coverage to increase its addressable market for Wi-Fi services. This is a new growth initiative.
metrics.market_note: Expanding FWA coverage to increase the addressable market is a positive growth initiative that the market is likely to view favorably.

key_takeaways: Bharti Airtel is showing improved EBITDA margins, driven by operational efficiencies and consolidation of Indus Towers.
The company is actively managing its portfolio by exiting low-margin B2B businesses to focus on higher-growth digital services.
Airtel is emphasizing cost reduction through initiatives like solarization and AI-powered network optimization.
Capex is expected to moderate in the coming fiscal year, contributing to improved free cash flow.
The company is changing the way they report margins, now reporting EBITDAaL, which is EBITDA after lease obligations.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: India revenues, excluding Indus, came in at Rs.34,900 Crores and the EBITDAaL margin excluding the passive infra side came in at 51.5%.
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: EBITDA margins came in at 56.2% an improvement of 1.4%. Effective Q3, Indus Towers has now consolidated into our financials.
metrics.language_shift: The company reports an increase in EBITDA margins, partially attributed to the consolidation of Indus Towers.
metrics.market_note: An increase in EBITDA margins, even partially attributed to the consolidation of Indus Towers, is a positive sign of improved profitability.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: India revenues, excluding Indus, came in at Rs.34,900 Crores and the EBITDAaL margin excluding the passive infra side came in at 51.5%.
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: This quarter, we have made an improvement to our disclosures. We are now reporting EBITDAaL, which is EBITDA after lease obligations. We believe this is a true reflection of our underlying margin and leverage. As a management team, this is a metric we track so as to drive the right behavior about the underlying health of the business. EBITDAaL for the quarter stood at 16,306 Crores with a margin of 49.3%.
metrics.language_shift: Airtel is now reporting EBITDAaL (EBITDA after lease obligations) as a key metric, providing a more comprehensive view of underlying margin and leverage.
metrics.market_note: Reporting EBITDAaL as a key metric is classified as noise. It's difficult to assess market alignment without knowing if analysts find this metric particularly useful or insightful.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: The fifth and last pillar of our strategy is war on waste. We continue to identify areas to drive efficiencies in our cost structure powered by extensive use of technology. For example, we have deployed AI/ML-powered RAN energy-saving tool that has enhanced our energy efficiency by optimizing the RAN operational cost. We believe there is still opportunity to strip out waste from our operations through a combination of using digital tools, extreme de-averaging, and very tightly run operations.
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: The fifth and last pillar of our strategy is war on waste. This is now ingrained in our ways of working. With the rigor to drive efficiency over the last few years, we have eliminated about $2 billion of wasteful expenditure. At the same time, there is still significant headroom to strip our waste further in several areas: network, sales, and even marketing.
metrics.language_shift: The company emphasizes the success of its 'war on waste' initiative, quantifying the amount of wasteful expenditure eliminated and highlighting ongoing efforts to further improve efficiency.
metrics.market_note: Emphasizing the success of the 'war on waste' initiative and quantifying the amount of wasteful expenditure eliminated is a positive sign of efficiency improvements.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: A very significant part of this is very low-margin business and, in fact, what it does is it clutters management focus, it has a lot of people running around trying to strike deals, and the margins we make here are insignificant. We have made a decision to exit this low-margin business, which will have an impact on the topline in the coming quarters. It will take about six months for this to play out, but let me underscore that the exit in this business will have really no impact on EBITDA because this is a very negligible margin business.
metrics.language_shift: Airtel is exiting low-margin B2B businesses to improve overall profitability and focus on high-growth digital adjacencies.
metrics.market_note: Exiting low-margin B2B businesses to improve overall profitability is a positive strategic move that the market is likely to support.

metrics.subtopic: Dilution in EBITDA margin due to converged solutions
metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: Soumen Ray – Chief Financial Officer - India & South Asia – Bharti Airtel Limited & Director, Bharti Hexacom Limited: So the reduction that you tell is because we have moved to selling converged solutions: where there is connectivity, there is services and there is also hardware bundled into it. For example let us take security: When we do a security deal, there is security services as well as security license, so, unlike connectivity, which is entirely our product some of these products has resell component built into it. So, that is why you see some dilution in EBITDA margin over these last nine months. You understand that connectivity is of course growing, but these kinds of products are growing much faster and hence their weightage will keep increasing so, to that extent, there will be an impact.
metrics.language_shift: CFO explains that the shift to selling converged solutions (connectivity, services, hardware) is causing some dilution in EBITDA margin because some of these products have a resell component built into it.
metrics.market_note: The signal suggests that selling converged solutions is causing some dilution in EBITDA margin. This is divergent with the general positive sentiment around EBITDA margin improvement. The market may view this negatively if the dilution is significant.

key_takeaways: Bharti Airtel is shifting focus towards deleveraging, increasing dividends, and making selective investments in B2B and digital areas.
The company emphasizes the need for further tariff repair to ensure financial stability and reasonable returns.
Airtel is exiting the low-margin commodity voice and messaging business to improve focus and drive growth in digital adjacencies.
Bharti Airtel is leveraging AI to optimize network capacity and reduce energy consumption.
Bharti Hexacom anticipates increased capex due to FWA rollout, particularly the higher cost of FWA CPE.

metrics.quote_old: At some stage, once the land grab phase is over, will tariffs have some correction or see some repair in this segment, given the value that we offer? The answer to that could be yes. I think today, it is a bit early to comment on that.
metrics.quote_new: Tariff repair is needed some more for the industry to be financially stable and deliver reasonable returns on a sustained basis.
metrics.language_shift: The narrative shifts from a potential future tariff repair to a current need for tariff repair to ensure financial stability. The tone is more assertive about the necessity of tariff adjustments.
metrics.market_note: The narrative shifts from a potential future tariff repair to a current need for tariff repair to ensure financial stability. The tone is more assertive about the necessity of tariff adjustments, which aligns with the market's understanding that tariff increases are crucial for improving profitability in the telecom sector.

metrics.quote_old: Ankur, on 5G standalone, there is really no meaningful capex, because it is all software-led, right? All our networks are capable of moving to standalone at the flick of a button, so it is a very, very tiny amount of money that we spend for software. On homes capex, it is all built into our overall guidance of moderating on capex, so, nothing changes there. The only question is on data centers. I think there is an opportunity here to step up the growth of data centers in terms of investment. That part is the part that we will assess fully and then sort of come back on how that will impact. My own sense is, it will not be a very meaningful change. There could be some bump-up because of the investment in Nxtra, but there again, I would say, it is a bit premature to talk about it because we will do what it takes to make sure that we put in the requisite investments to build our position strongly. There, it is a very good investment because you get firm contracts for 20 - 25 years and so, to that extent, that works well.
metrics.quote_new: We believe that our capex for the current year will be lower than FY2024, and this will continue to unwind in FY2026.
metrics.language_shift: The company provides a clearer outlook on capex, stating it will be lower than FY2024 and continue to decrease in FY2026. This is a more definitive statement compared to the previous quarter's discussion around potential data center investments.
metrics.market_note: The company provides a clearer outlook on capex, stating it will be lower than FY2024 and continue to decrease in FY2026. This is a more definitive statement compared to the previous quarter's discussion around potential data center investments, which aligns with the market's positive view on lower capex.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: Opportunities for free cash flow, I think, will be some amount of deleveraging, which will continue as you also pointed out. There will be a step-up of dividend for sure and that is a second area of free cash flow and the third, we will be selective and very prudent in any investments we make.
metrics.language_shift: The company explicitly outlines its priorities for free cash flow: deleveraging, increased dividends, and prudent investments. This provides more clarity on capital allocation strategy.
metrics.market_note: The company explicitly outlines its priorities for free cash flow: deleveraging, increased dividends, and prudent investments. This provides more clarity on capital allocation strategy, which aligns with the market's preference for balanced capital allocation.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: We have made a decision to exit this low-margin business, which will have an impact on the topline in the coming quarters. It will take about six months for this to play out, but let me underscore that the exit in this business will have really no impact on EBITDA because this is a very negligible margin business.
metrics.language_shift: Airtel announces its decision to exit the low-margin commodity voice and messaging business. This strategic shift is aimed at improving management focus and driving growth in digital adjacencies.
metrics.market_note: Airtel announces its decision to exit the low-margin commodity voice and messaging business. This strategic shift is aimed at improving management focus and driving growth in digital adjacencies, which aligns with the market's support for strategic moves to improve profitability.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: As a result, we are able to turn off layers of radio technology based on real time traffic patterns, which reduce our carbon footprint and also lower our energy bill.
metrics.language_shift: Airtel highlights its use of AI to optimize network capacity, reduce carbon footprint, and lower energy bills. This showcases a focus on efficiency and sustainability.
metrics.market_note: Airtel highlights its use of AI to optimize network capacity, reduce carbon footprint, and lower energy bills. This showcases a focus on efficiency and sustainability, which aligns with the market's increasing interest in ESG factors.

metrics.quote_old: Not discussed in previous quarter
metrics.quote_new: We must also remember that this FWA rollout will create a need for capex in Hexacom, because this FWA CPE is significantly more expensive than the broadband CPE, so, there will be some need of additional investments, but yes, the net debt to EBITDA looks very promising at just above one and as you rightly said that if we get this money, it will further come down. Dividend payout is something which will be certainly looked at with a positive angle.
metrics.language_shift: Bharti Hexacom faces increased capex needs due to FWA rollout, particularly the higher cost of FWA CPE compared to broadband CPE. This is a new disclosure regarding specific capex pressures within Hexacom.
metrics.validation_note: The quote mentions "net debt to EBITDA looks very promising at just above one" which is positive, not negative. The signal should be positive, not negative.
metrics.market_note: Bharti Hexacom faces increased capex needs due to FWA rollout, particularly the higher cost of FWA CPE compared to broadband CPE. This is a new disclosure regarding specific capex pressures within Hexacom, which is divergent with the general positive sentiment around lower capex.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: Let me start with an update on the AGR issue. We have always maintained that the AGR judgment of 2019 was a body blow to the industry. The fact that even errors of calculation were not entertained was even more disappointing. We welcome the fact that the recent order of the Supreme Court permits the government to undertake a comprehensive assessment, reassessment, and reconciliation of the AGR dues, including interest and penalties, up to financial year 2016-2017. The order has been made in the petition of Vodafone Idea. We are now planning to take up our matter with the government.
metrics.quote_new: No longer discussed
metrics.language_shift: The AGR issue, which was a key topic in the previous quarter, is no longer discussed. This could indicate that there are no significant updates or developments to report, or that the company is shifting focus to other areas.
metrics.market_note: The AGR issue, which was a key topic in the previous quarter, is no longer discussed. This could indicate that there are no significant updates or developments to report, or that the company is shifting focus to other areas. Without knowing the market's prior expectations or views on this issue, it's difficult to validate.

metrics.quote_old: Gopal Vittal – Vice Chairman & Managing Director - Bharti Airtel Limited: I think, firstly, 6G is too far out even 5G has been across the world also shares the GSMA, across the Telco world, I can tell you that, one of the pain points in the telecom world across the globe is that 5G has not lived its promise of what it was meant to. The primary use case of 5G is only speed and now it is just a more efficient way of producing the same gigabyte, it is a more efficient way of doing it, but it has not led to any monetization, anywhere in the world.
metrics.quote_new: No longer discussed
metrics.language_shift: The lack of 5G monetization, a concern in the previous quarter, is no longer discussed. This could indicate a shift in focus or a lack of new developments in this area.
metrics.market_note: The lack of 5G monetization, a concern in the previous quarter, is no longer discussed. This could indicate a shift in focus or a lack of new developments in this area. Without knowing the market's prior expectations or views on this issue, it's difficult to validate.

summary: Bharti Airtel is prioritizing deleveraging and financial prudence, demonstrated by the prepayment of high-cost spectrum dues. Capex is expected to moderate in FY2026 as a percentage of revenues, driven by the completion of major 5G rollouts. Bharti Airtel is focusing on acquiring quality customers in various segments and retooling its B2B segment by exiting low-margin businesses.
